data.narrative: This module moved from observability design into real proof. You implemented hybrid structured logging, Prometheus metrics, and alert definitions, then used live smoke checks and a controlled failure route to prove what worked and to isolate a real cardinality bug in the failure-path metrics. What stood out most was the way you corrected misleading signals with runtime evidence instead of trusting assumptions.

dimension_scores.observation: Containerization knowledge stayed stable in the background while you focused on observability work.
dimension_scores.evidence: Your work this module centered on logging, /metrics, and alerting rather than changing the Docker image or runtime container structure.

dimension_scores.observation: Pipeline thinking stayed competent but was not the main surface exercised in this module.
dimension_scores.evidence: You verified observability locally through live requests and metrics output rather than extending the CI/CD workflow itself.

dimension_scores.observation: You implemented all three observability pillars and proved them with real output, while also noticing the difference between metric existence and metric truthfulness.
dimension_scores.evidence: You reported that the live /metrics output included labeled http_requests_total and populated http_request_duration_seconds buckets, and you correctly noted that '0.0 is still just the placeholder value from URLS_TOTAL.set(0), not a real database-backed count yet.'

dimension_scores.observation: You traced a subtle production-style bug from symptom to code path and explained why it would hurt only under failure conditions.
dimension_scores.evidence: You said, 'The likely bug is in the exception path using raw_route instead of the matched route template, so failed requests on dynamic URLs create unique metric labels while successful requests mostly use normalized route templates.'

dimension_scores.observation: You showed solid awareness of preserving all three observability pillars during a fix, though graceful degradation itself was not yet the focus of the module.
dimension_scores.evidence: You summarized the fix outcome as 'Logs still include structured request context, failure metrics still increment with normalized labels, and the error-rate alert math still works because status-code counting behavior did not change.'

dimension_scores.observation: Your alert-definition proof was concise and complete, covering the parts an on-call engineer would need.
dimension_scores.evidence: You enumerated HighErrorRate, HighLatency, and ServiceDown with expression, threshold, for duration, severity, human-readable message, and runbook link.

strengths: Strong evidence-driven debugging: you repeatedly verified live behavior instead of trusting assumptions, for example proving the cardinality fix from real /metrics output and proving the 500 error path with a temporary /boom route.
Strong observability reasoning: you distinguished between a metric existing and a metric telling the truth when you called out urls_total 0.0 as only a placeholder rather than a real business value.
growth_areas: The business gauge is still placeholder-backed rather than database-backed; you explicitly noted that '0.0 is still just the placeholder value from URLS_TOTAL.set(0), not a real database-backed count yet.'
You needed substantial scaffolding to translate the metrics design into code-ready implementation steps, especially around route normalization and proof sequencing.
data.recommendation: In Module 5, keep the same habit of proving each failure mode with a live check before trusting the symptom description, and make the first business metric fully truth-backed rather than placeholder-backed whenever possible.

public_evidence.artifact: API observability surface in apps/api/app/main.py and apps/api/app/logging_utils.py
public_evidence.student_action: Implemented hybrid structured logging, Prometheus request metrics, a /metrics endpoint, and verified them with live requests plus a controlled 500 failure route.
public_evidence.technical_detail: FastAPI middleware now emits structured request logs with request_id and records Prometheus counters and histograms labeled by method, normalized route, and status_code.
public_evidence.proof: Live development log line included timestamp, level, message, service_name, and request_id; live /metrics output showed labeled http_requests_total and populated http_request_duration_seconds buckets; a temporary /boom route returned 500 and produced an ERROR log with request_id and stack trace.
public_evidence.ability: Can instrument a service for production observability and verify the instrumentation from real runtime output.
public_evidence.reviewer_value: Shows the student can add logging and metrics that are useful in an incident, not just write feature code.

public_evidence.artifact: Prometheus metric label strategy for dynamic API routes
public_evidence.student_action: Diagnosed and fixed a cardinality bug by normalizing failure-path route labels instead of emitting raw dynamic paths.
public_evidence.technical_detail: The failure metric path now uses the matched route template rather than raw_route, preventing one time series per dynamic ID.
public_evidence.proof: After hitting multiple dynamic failing routes, /metrics showed one normalized route label: route="/teams/{team_id}/members" instead of separate raw paths.
public_evidence.ability: Can recognize and fix an observability scaling bug before it becomes a production incident.
public_evidence.reviewer_value: Demonstrates mature metrics judgment, especially around label cardinality and operational cost.

concepts_demonstrated.evidence: You verified the live request log contained timestamp, level, message, service_name, and request_id and explained why hybrid formatting helps both local debugging and production aggregation.
You explained that separate route labels like /links/a1 and /links/b2 would prove a cardinality blow-up, and you fixed the exception path to use normalized route templates.
You answered that too many alerts are worse because people start ignoring or muting them, which makes real incidents easier to miss.

teaching_approaches.detail: Used concrete live checks for /health, /metrics, and a temporary failing /boom route instead of abstract explanation alone.
Had the student predict what metric lines would look like if route labels exploded, then compare that mental model to the actual code and runtime output.

learning_style_signals: Benefits from exact proof formats and command-level verification once the concept is understood.
Responds especially well to comparing observed runtime output against a predicted failure pattern.
